coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots for visual alcohol detection and light-emitting diodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Qiuchun
Huang, Fengchang
Tao, Xiaoma
Ouyang, Yifang
Zhou, Yulu
Chen, Ping
Mo, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) is highly desired in developing full-color solid-state lighting and display applications based on CDs. Developing simple and eco-friendly methods to produce R-CDs is extremely desirable. In this work, two types of R-CDs were obtained via simple and eco-friendly grinding-heating or solvothermal methods using fresh spinach leaves as the carbon source, respectively. The obtained R-CDs naturally possess surface functional groups. These R-CDs were effectively used as sensitive probes for detecting alcohol (including alcohol gas and liquor detection) due to the synergistic effect of the nitrogen-containing surface groups on the R-CDs and the oxhydryl groups in the alcohol solution. Finally, red electroluminescence was achieved by mixing the R-CDs with polyvinyl pyrrolidone as the phosphor layer on a blue light-emitting diode (LED) chip. This work paves the way for the further development of R-CDs in fluorescence probes and full-color LED applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Light-emitting diode
Electroluminescence
Spinach
Alcohol detection

Thank Miss Xiangyu Wei, Miss Xiaoqi Ma, and Miss Maolan Peng for their wonderful help in the experiments and tests. Special appreciation to Prof. Jialong Zhaos special fund of \"Guangxi Bagui Scholar\".

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme for preparing the GRI-CDs and SOL-CDs. Glass vials A and B show photos of the R-CDs in DI water and ethanol, respectively, illuminated by 365 nm UV light.
(a)–(b) TEM and HRTEM images for GRI-CDs, and (c)–(d) for SOL-CDs in ethanol. (e) FTIR spectra of the R-CDs in ethanol.

UV–visible absorption (left) and excitation-dependent PL spectra (right) for (a) GRI-CDs, (b) SOL-CDs in ethanol and (c) ethanol extract. PLE spectra with emission at 676 nm for (d) GRI-CDs and (e) SOL-CDs and (f) ethanol extract. XRD of different materials (g) and distribution of (h) PL and (i) PL intensity and peak position of the SOL-CDs under continuous irradiation at 420 nm for 9 h.

(a) PL spectra at different alcohol concentrations. (b) Fluorescence brightness (the inset is the fluorescence photograph at each concentration, from left to right, 0 %–100 %). (c) The distribution of each PL spectrum in CIE coordinates. (d) Diagram showing the use of the R-CDs for detecting ethanol and alcohol (aqueous) in Chinese liquor. Typical photos of corresponding procedures for (e) the GRI-CDs and (f) the SOL-CDs.

(a) Diagram showing the use of the SOL-CDs for detecting alcohol (gas) in different alcoholic beverages with alcohol contents (% by vol.) available on the market. (b)–(e) Typical detection photos for the Tsingtao Beer, Rio Cocktail, Redstar Chinese Liquor, and ethanol.

(a)–(b) Current and luminance characteristics as a function of the applied forward bias for the red LEDs. The insets show the corresponding device EQEs concerning the applied bias. (c)–(d) PL of the red CDs/PVP-based phosphors and EL spectra of the red LEDs. The insets show corresponding EL photos when driven at 3.4 V.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of red-emissive carbon dots for visual alcohol detection and light-emitting diodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center on Nanoenergy Research, Guangxi Key Laboratory for Relativistic Astrophysics, School of Physical Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangxi Key Laboratory for Relativistic Astrophysics
School of Physical Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center on Nanoenergy Research, Guangxi Key Laboratory for Relativistic Astrophysics, School of Physical Science and Technology, Guangxi University, Nanning, 530004, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red-emissive carbon dots (R-CDs) is highly desired in developing full-color solid-state lighting and display applications based on CDs. Developing simple and eco-friendly methods to produce R-CDs is extremely desirable. In this work, two types of R-CDs were obtained via simple and eco-friendly grinding-heating or solvothermal methods using fresh spinach leaves as the carbon source, respectively. The obtained R-CDs naturally possess surface functional groups. These R-CDs were effectively used as sensitive probes for detecting alcohol (including alcohol gas and liquor detection) due to the synergistic effect of the nitrogen-containing surface groups on the R-CDs and the oxhydryl groups in the alcohol solution. Finally, red electroluminescence was achieved by mixing the R-CDs with polyvinyl pyrrolidone as the phosphor layer on a blue light-emitting diode (LED) chip. This work paves the way for the further development of R-CDs in fluorescence probes and full-color LED applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As an emerging class of nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable research interest due to their stable luminescence, good biocompatibility, and environmental friendliness. Owing to the quantum confinement effect or conjugated π-domains, CDs exhibit tunable photoluminescence (PL) and excitation-dependent multicolor emission. These distinct properties make CDs suitable for a wide range of applications, including analyte detection, bioimaging, solar cells, optoelectronic devices, and so forth [1–10]. Since the discovery in 2004 [11], CDs have been easily synthesized through two traditional methods: top-down and bottom-up methods [12,13]. The bottom-up approach typically involves using large organic molecules, such as anhydrous citric acid, poly (vinyl alcohol), and phenylene diamines as the precursor, which suffer from the issues of thermal decomposition, combustion and dehydration [14–17]. The top-down approach usually employs macroscopic carbon structure as the carbon source, which are broken down into nanocarbon to generate desired CDs [18,19]. It is not difficult to find that the existing synthesis processes are complex, to some extent, and the carbon sources used are often slightly toxic. Therefore, developing a simple and eco-friendly approach to produce CDs efficiently and massively is highly desirable for the development of CD-based devices. In 2017, Li et al. utilized green plant spinach as the carbon source to produce red fluorescent CDs with a PL quantum yield (PLQY) of 15.34 % and good biocompatibility through solvothermal carbonization [20]. Subsequently, CDs were synthesized from various plant sources, including pomelo peel, plant leaves, trapa bispinosa, aloe, and gardenia [20–25]. These CDs showed strong fluorescence and outstanding stability, highlighting the great potential of preparing CDs using convenient and eco-friendly carbon precursors originated from green plants.

Red phosphors are indispensable components for fabricating full-color light emission and display devices based on CDs. However, most CDs typically emit in the blue to green spectral regions [1,15,16]. Therefore, achieving highly efficient red-emissive CDs (R-CDs) is of significant importance for further full-color applications. For the fluorescence mechanism of CDs, it has been reported that the red fluorescence of CDs may originates from their surface states, quantum confinement effect, defect states and π-π transitions [26]. Consequently, the most common method for realizing red fluorescence involves creating surface defects (e.g. the functional groups containing nitrogen, oxygen, or fluorine) by adding heteroatoms, leading to considerable surface-state related red fluorescence [27,28]. Additionally, altering the size of the CDs could also influence the quantum confinement effects, narrowing the band energy and inducing long-wavelength emission [5,29].

Here in this study, we focus on the synthesis of red fluorescent CDs using green plant spinach leaves as the carbon source. By utilizing spinach leaves, we aim to develop a sustainable and green method for producing high-efficiency R-CDs. Leveraging the inherent properties of spinach leaves, such as nitrogen-containing compounds and chlorophyll related groups, two kinds of R-CDs were successfully prepared using the grinding-heating method and solvothermal methods, respectively. The R-CDs exhibited red emission centered at approximately 676 nm and 728 nm in ethanol solution, with the highest PLQY up to 27 %. Based on the dependence of fluorescence intensity, luma and CIE coordinates on ethanol concentration, we realized the potential of the R-CDs as a novel testing probe for ethanol detection, offering a distinct approach compared to current technologies for alcohol detection. Furthermore, red light-emitting diodes (LEDs) were produced using the R-CDs as phosphors by blending them with polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP). Our findings demonstrate the great potential of the eco-friendly R-CDs in multifunctional applications.

Synthesis of the R-CDs. The R-CDs were produced by a one-pot, eco-friendly synthesis using leafy vegetables, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . Spinach was chosen as the raw material due to its luxuriant leaves with a high content of chlorophyll. After being purchased from the local market, the spinach was thoroughly cleaned. The leaves were carefully removed, soaked in deionized water for 20 min, and then ground into juice in a glass mortar with the aid of adequate deionized water. The spinach juice was completely dried at a constant temperature of 80 °C, and the resulting solids were ground into powder. The prepared spinach powder (10 g) was dissolved in 60 mL of ethanol and stirred constantly for 40 min at room temperature. The solution was then filtered several times to remove any undissolved granules. The filtered solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave for solvothermal treatment at 180 °C for 8 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the resulting dark brown solution was dried at 80 °C under vacuum. The resultant CDs powder was redispersed in ethanol to obtain a solvothermal CDs solution, denoted as SOL-CDs. For comparison, another 10 g of the prepared spinach powder was dissolved in 60 mL of ethanol (same as the SOL-CDs), but after filtration to remove the undissolved granules, the solution was stirred for over 12 h at 80 °C until it appeared red under 365 nm UV lamp illumination (without solvothermal treatment). The resulting solution was dried under vacuum, and the resultant CDs powder was redispersed in ethanol to form another solution, denoted as GRI-CDs. The ethanol extract of spinach was obtained by grinding and filtering the spinach leaves with ethanol.

Preparation of red LEDs based on SOL-CDs and GRI-CDs. 12 mg of the SOL-CDs powder was dispersed in 3 mL ethanol. To prevent luminescence quenching due to agglomeration, 23 mg of highly transparent polymer matrix PVP (K90) was added as a dispersing agent. The mixture was then dried, and the resultant solid was ground to obtain SOL-CDs/PVP phosphors. Afterward, 25 mg of SOL-CDs/PVP phosphors were redissolved in 0.1 mL ethanol and thoroughly mixed with a thermally curable silicone resin A and the hardener B (in a mass ratio of A: B to be 0.15 g: 0.15 g). The resulting composite was promptly transferred to a vacuum oven as soon as possible to remove the bubbles inside the composite by heating it at 60 °C for 30 min. Finally, the composite containing the SOL-CDs/PVP phosphors was carefully applied on a 460 nm blue LED chip and cured at 120 °C for 2 h to obtain a blue-excited red LED device based on SOL-CDs. For comparison, GRI-CDs powder was also used as the phosphors combined with a 460 nm blue LED chip to produce another blue-excited red LED. The manufacturing process for this LED was identical to that of the LED based on SOL-CDs.

Characterizations. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) observations were performed in FEI Talos F200. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured using a Cary 660-FT-IR spectrometer. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded via a UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 950). The absolute PLQYs of the GRI- and SOL-CDs were measured in Edinburgh Instruments FLS1000 spectrometer equipped with a 120 mm integrating sphere. The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and PL spectrum of the GRI- and SOL-CDs in ethanol were collected by a Zolix monochromator coupled with a photomultiplier tube (PMT) as the detector. The PL of the GRI-CDs/PVP and SOL-CDs/PVP phosphors as well as the electroluminescence (EL) spectra of the LEDs were measured at room temperature by the same Zolix monochromator using an Andor CCD detector. Current-voltage (I–V) characteristics and luminance of the LEDs were measured by a Keithley 2400 source meter and a KONICA MINOLTA luminance meter (LS-150), respectively. The external quantum efficiencies (EQEs) of the LEDs were calculated by testing the output optical power with a silicon photodetector coupled to an optical power meter (PS100, CNI Optoelectronics Technology). Photographs were taken by using a Canon EOS 70D digital camera. X-ray diffraction (XRD) were collected by Tongda TD-3500 with Cu Kα radiation.

Using plant-based carbon sources for synthesizing CDs is a cost-effective, renewable, and environmentally friendly approach. This method leverages abundant and biocompatible materials, enhancing PL properties and simplifying synthesis process. Herein, we prepare highly efficient R-CDs using spinach leaves as carbon source. As shown in Fig. 1, two kinds of R-CDs, namely grinding-heating treated GRI-CDs and solvothermal treated SOL-CDs, are successfully obtained. Both types of R-CDs exhibit excellent dispersion in water and ethanol due to the presence of large hydrophilic groups. In this process, spinach leaves serve as the carbon source and provide surface states for the fluorescent R-CDs. In ethanol solvent, the R-CDs exhibit strong red fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Xu studied the effect of solvents on the fluorescence of CDs [30], noting that solvents lacking –OH and –COOH functional groups induce a red shift in the spectrum, whereas those containing these groups will enhance fluorescence intensity. When dispersed in water, CDs experience fluorescence quenching due to the deprotonation of amino and hydroxyl groups on their surfaces, resulting in negligible fluorescence in aqueous environments but bright red emission in ethanol. Consequently, these CDs can serve as effective probes for the rapid detection of alcohol molecules, with reversible responses to ethanol. Fig. 2 a and c presents typical TEM images and the size distributions of the synthesized GRI-CDs and SOL-CDs, respectively. The GRI-CDs exhibit spherical shapes with particle sizes ranging from 2.5 to 3.5 nm, whereas the SOL-CDs have larger sizes ranging from 4.5 to 5.5 nm due to the solvothermal process promoting CDs growth. HRTEM images (Fig. 2b and d) reveal lattice spacings of 0.21 nm, consistent with the graphite (100) facet, suggesting a graphitic framework structure for the CDs [29]. GRI-CDs prepared via grinding and heating rely on mechanical force and low temperatures to form CDs, thus resulting in limited aggregation and smaller sizes. The gradual carbonization process at low temperatures further inhibits dot growth, leading to smaller sizes and many uncarbonized molecules, and lattice fringes also can be observed in self-assembled conjugated molecules. Therefore, the lattice fringes may come from CDs or self-assembled molecules. Conversely, SOL-CDs, similar to the reported by Li et al. [20], are produced via the solvothermal treatment at 180 °C. This method facilitates the rapid formation and growth of larger CDs under high temperatures and pressure. Furthermore, ethanol and other solvents can promote aggregation, thereby increasing the size of the CDs. All these conditions accelerate the carbonization processes, creating favorable conditions for the growth and aggregation of the CDs, ultimately yielding larger CDs. The R-CDs are further characterized by FT-IR (Fig. 2e) to investigate the composition of their surface-modifying functional groups in the ethanol solution. The two types of R-CDs are highly similar in FT-IR spectra, indicating the existence of similar functional groups or chemical bonds, including stretching vibrations of C–H (∼2985 cm−1), C=O (∼1686 cm−1), C=N (∼1619 cm−1), C=C (∼1502 cm−1), C–N (∼1393 cm−1) and C–O (∼1247 cm−1) [31,32]. These observations indicate the presence of nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on the R-CD surfaces, potentially interacting with the carbon core and creating emissive traps.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of GRI-CDs and SOL-CDs are collected, as shown in Fig. 3 a and (b), respectively. The absorption spectra of both R-CDs are similar, with the absorption region extending from UV to visible light. Both R-CDs show absorption peaks at 312, 374, 410, 506, 539, 607 and 666 nm. The bands at 374 and 410 nm are attributed to the n-π* electron transition of C=O bands from different surface oxygen-containing functional groups, while the band at 312 nm corresponds to the π-π* transitions of C=C and C=N bonds in the carbon core [33–35]. These features are generated after the grinding-heating/solvothermal process from spinach leaves and the subsequent carbonization process during the preparation of the R-CDs. Consistent with the absorption spectrum, the PLE spectrum of GRI-CDs has peaks at 386, 423, 504, 544, 607, and 667 nm (Fig. 3d). In addition, there is a weak PLE peak at 470 nm which is not shown in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum due to its weak absorption intensity. Similarly, PLE spectrum of the SOL-CDs shows the absorption peak positions at 366, 428, 470, 504, 540, 608, and 670 nm (Fig. 3e). The peak at 366 nm deviates from that of the GRI-CDs by 20 nm, likely owing to the different environments of the C=O chemical bond or functional group on the surface of the two types of R-CDs. The strongest excitation wavelength of the GRI-CDs occurs at 420 nm, while for the SOL-CDs it is at 500 nm. The emission wavelength and intensity dependence on the excitation wavelength is a common phenomenon observed in carbon-based fluorescent materials [36,37]. These behaviors may reflect not only effects from particles of different sizes in the sample but also the distribution of different surface functional groups on each nanoparticle [24]. However, there are some significant differences in optical properties between the as-prepared R-CDs from spinach and the traditional CDs. When excited in the range of 320–420 nm, both types of R-CDs exhibit strong red fluorescence, with two peaks in the PL spectra centered at 676 and 728 nm (Fig. 3a and b). When the excitation wavelength is further increased, the emission centers of the R-CDs remain constant. In other words, the as-prepared spinach-based R-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission behavior, which is inconsistent with most of the previous reports on traditional CDs. The excitation-independent emission of the spinach-based R-CDs implies that surface state of the R-CDs should be single and uniform distribution [38]. And a well-documented dependence of its PL intensity on excitation wavelength has been observed due to the uneven distribution of the CDs’ size (as shown in Fig. 2a and c). However, the trend of this intensity change corresponds to the PLE spectrum. The PLQY of the GRI-CDs and SOL-CDs was 27 % and 19 % under 380 nm excitation, respectively. The PL spectra of the two types of R-CDs also differ from the previously reported chlorophyll luminescence spectra but are consistent with those of spinach CDs reported earlier [20,39]. To explore the source of R-CDs fluorescence, the ethanol extract of spinach is characterized. As shown in Fig. 3c and f, the ethanol extract has a continuous absorption band from 200 to 500 nm, and the PLE spectrum ranges from 250 to 500 nm. Ethanol extract of spinach contains a variety of different compounds, resulting in the overall absorption spectrum with the continuous band. The PL is centered at 676 nm with a small shoulder peak at 728 nm, and the emission center is independent of the excitation wavelength. This indicates that the red fluorescence of spinach-based CDs originates from the modification of natural molecules in spinach. In contrast, GRI-CDs exhibit distinct absorption and PLE features that are different from spinach ethanol extract, but it is similar to the SOL-CDs, showing significant characteristic peaks that confirm the formation of CD core. Feng et al. successfully synthesized fluorescent CDs using sucrose as a carbon source and phosphoric acid and ammonium hydroxide as oxidizers and passivators under mild conditions at 80 °C [40]. Therefore, it is plausible that GRI-CDs contain a fraction of CDs. During the low-temperature grinding and stirring process, self-assembled conjugated molecules likely form, which may also influence the fluorescence of the GRI-CDs. Additionally, we observe an XRD peak around 28° in the prepared R-CDs (Fig. 3g), which is also present in the ethanol extract but absent in spinach powder. This indicates that the formation of new carbonaceous structures during the preparation process. To test the stability of the synthesized R-CDs, we studied the photobleaching resistance of SOL-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3h, under continuous 360 nm light irradiation, the PL spectrum of the SOL-CDs remains stable, with only slight fluctuations in intensity and no change in the luminescence center (as shown in Fig. 3i), indicating that the SOL-CDs have good photobleaching resistance.

The –OH and –COOH groups in many organic solvents can enhance the fluorescence of CDs. Here, we take ethanol as an example to explore the fluorescence characteristics of R-CDs in different ethanol concentrations. As shown in Fig. 4 a, when the same amount of SOL-CDs are dissolved in aqueous solutions with different ethanol contents, the PL intensity increases with the rise in ethanol ratio. Its fluorescence under UV light is shown in the inset of Fig. 4b. The fluorescence luma calculated from the PL spectrum also increases with the enhancement of the ethanol concentration, and the CIE coordinates shift to the red region. Exploiting the fluorescence dependence of the synthesized R-CDs in ethanol aqueous solution indicates that the R-CDs can be used as probes for alcohol detection. As shown in Fig. 4d, the as-prepared R-CDs solution was painted on letters “G”, “X”, and “U”, which were cut from sketch paper. Under 365 nm UV excitation, the letters exhibit red fluorescence, consistent with the PL properties of the R-CDs. After natural drying, its fluorescence under 365 nm UV fades and disappeares as the alcohol is evaporated. When ethanol or liquor is dropped on the letters again, the red fluorescence of the R-CDs is restored. This phenomenon suggests that alcohol prevents the accumulation of R-CDs and avoids π–π stacking in the solid state, which consumes the transition energy and prevents the aggregation from large conjugated systems. For the SOL-CDs, the red fluorescence is more pronounced when ethanol is reapplied than the letters with the GRI-CDs. This indicates that although SOL-CDs are not purified, CDs are still synthesized during the solvothermal process, and a bit remaining small molecules do not affect the luminescence properties of the CDs in ethanol. However, For the GRI-CDs obtained by grinding-heating and drying, much smaller number of CDs are carbonized, so the GRI-CDs test paper do not respond significantly to alcohol. Therefore, the SOL-CDs are more sensitive in alcohol detection and can be further used for alcohol gas detection, as shown in Fig. 5 . The “G”, “X”, and “U” letters containing the SOL-CDs were soaked in water and then placed over a large beaker containing the Tsingtao Beer (4 % vol.), Rio Cock (8 % vol.), Redstar Liquor (56 % vol.), and pure ethanol, respectively. After the alcohol has volatilized for 30 min, the alcohol gas is absorbed and adhered to the letters, and the sketch paper displays red fluorescence when excited under 365 nm UV illumination. The red fluorescence becomes more pronounced with the increase of alcohol concentration, as shown in Fig. 5b to e. The higher the alcohol concentration, the more alcohol evaporates and adheres to the detection probe at the same time, resulting in more obvious red fluorescence.

The high fluorescence efficiency of the CDs-based phosphors enables their application in optoelectronic devices, especially LEDs. In this work, R-CDs with red fluorescence are simply synthesized without any catalysts or further surface passivation. The R-CDs exhibit excellent photoluminescent properties and their red LED applications are demonstrated using the R-CDs as phosphors and 460 nm blue LED chips as the excitation source, as shown in Fig. 6 . For comparison, both GRI-CDs/PVP and SOL-CDs/PVP composite film-based red LED are prepared (see the I–V and L-V curves Fig. 6a and b). As shown in the PL spectra (Fig. 6c and d), both of the GRI-CDs/PVP and SOL-CDs/PVP composite films display a sharp emission peak centered at 676 nm under excitation of a 325 nm He–Cd laser, in addition to a blue peak at 437 nm (originating from PVP) and a green emission peak at 505 nm. Similar to our previous work, the green peak at 505 nm is likely attributed to the intrinsic state of the CDs core and the C=O surface groups [41]. Besides, a weaker shoulder peak at 728 nm can also be observed in the PL of the GRI-CDs/PVP composite film but disappears in the PL of the SOL-CDs/PVP composite film. However, the 728 nm red emission becomes more dominant in the EL spectra of GRI-CDs/PVP and SOL-CDs/PVP LEDs, causing the light emission of LEDs to appear red, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 6c and d. The PVP in the device prevents the fluorescence quenching caused by the agglomeration of the R-CDs, allowing the device to emit red EL. With increasing injection voltages, the luminance intensities of the both red LEDs increase significantly, but the external quantum efficiency (EQE) of the both red LEDs decreases (see the insets of Fig. 6a and b). Under the same forward bias injection, the EQE of SOL-CDs/PVP LEDs is higher, which indicates the better performance of the SOL-CDs/PVP composite film to convert blue light into red emission.

In summary, spinach leaves were used as the precursor to prepare red-emissive GRI-CDs and SOL-CDs. We developed an easy, eco-friendly, and economical route via grinding-heating and solvothermal processes to fabricate R-CDs for versatile applications. The as-prepared R-CDs were synthesized without any catalysts or surface passivation, and achieved red fluorescence in the alcohol solution with the PL centers at approximately 676 and 728 nm, with the highest PLQY up to 27 %. Red EL was obtained by designing a new LED device structure based on the R-CDs and PVP polymer. The red fluorescent properties of the prepared R-CDs in alcohol were utilized for alcohol detection, providing a new, simple, and green method for alcohol detection. Our findings demonstrate the potential multifunctional applications of the R-CDs produced by simple and eco-friendly methods.

Qiuchun Lu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Fengchang Huang: Data curation. Xiaoma Tao: Formal analysis. Yifang Ouyang: Software, Methodology. Yulu Zhou: Validation, Software, Resources, Funding acquisition. Ping Chen: Visualization, Supervision. Xiaoming Mo: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 11865004), and Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region (Grant No. 2018GXNSFBA281163, 2018GXNSFAA281309). Thank Miss Xiangyu Wei, Miss Xiaoqi Ma, and Miss Maolan Peng for their wonderful help in the experiments and tests. Special appreciation to Prof. Jialong Zhaos special fund of “Guangxi Bagui Scholar".

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Color-switchable electroluminescence of carbon dot light-emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Zhang, Y. Zhang, Y. Wang, S. Kalytchuk, S. V. Kershaw, Y. Wang, P. Wang, T. Zhang, Y. Zhao, H. Zhang, T. Cui, Y. Wang, J. Zhao, W. W. Yu, A. L. Rogach, Color-Switchable Electroluminescence of Carbon Dot Light-Emitting Diodes, ACS Nano 7 (2013) 11234-11241.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrahigh brightness carbon dot–based blue electroluminescent LEDs by host–guest energy transfer emission mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xu, Y. Miao, J. Zheng, Y. Yang, X. Liu, Ultrahigh Brightness Carbon Dot-Based Blue Electroluminescent LEDs by Host-Guest Energy Transfer Emission Mechanism, Advanced Optical Materials 6 (2018) 1800181.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dot nanothermometry: intracellular photoluminescence lifetime thermal sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Kalytchuk, K. Polakova, Y. Wang, J. P. Froning, K. Cepe, A. L. Rogach, R. Zboril, Carbon Dot Nanothermometry: Intracellular Photoluminescence Lifetime Thermal Sensing, ACS Nano 11 (2017) 1432-1442.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Förster resonance energy transfer and carbon dots enhance light harvesting in a solid-state quantum dot solar cell

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Narayanan, M. Deepa, A. K. Srivastava, Forster resonance energy transfer and carbon dots enhance light harvesting in a solid-state quantum dot solar cell, Journal of Materials Chemistry A 1 (2013) 3907-3918.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Full-color light-emitting carbon dots with a surface-state-controlled luminescence mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Ding, S.-B. Yu, J.-S. Wei, H.-M. Xiong, Full-Color Light-Emitting Carbon Dots with a Surface-State-Controlled Luminescence Mechanism, ACS Nano 10 (2016) 484-491.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Functional surface engineering of C-dots for fluorescent biosensing and in vivo bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Ding, A. Zhu, Y. Tian, Functional Surface Engineering of C-Dots for Fluorescent Biosensing and in Vivo Bioimaging, Accounts of Chemical Research 47 (2014) 20-30.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. T. Zheng, A. Ananthanarayanan, K. Q. Luo, P. Chen, Glowing Graphene Quantum Dots and Carbon Dots: Properties, Syntheses, and Biological Applications, Small 11 (2015) 1620-1636.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-Doped carbon dots for “green” quantum dot solar cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, P. Sun, S. Cong, J. Wu, L. Gao, Y. Wang, X. Dai, Q. Yi, G. Zou, Nitrogen-Doped Carbon Dots for “green” Quantum Dot Solar Cells, Nanoscale Research Letters 11 (2016) 27.
